title: Kundalini and Gratitude: The Unexpected Arrival of Thankfulness

body:
Kundalini and Gratitude: The Unexpected Arrival of Thankfulness

You're probably reading this because you're in it, aren't you? That feeling. That indescribable, terrifying, utterly alien experience that's ripped your life apart. You're alone, it's 3 AM, and your mind is racing, trying to make sense of the chaos. I know that feeling. I've been there. I've sat in that exact spot, heart pounding, body vibrating, feeling like I was losing my mind, or worse, dying. And you're probably thinking, "Gratitude? What the hell is Kalesh talking about? I'm barely holding on here!"

Look, I get it. When you're in the throes of a kundalini crisis, gratitude is the last thing on your mind. You're too busy trying to survive, trying to find a solid surface in a world that's turned to liquid. You're questioning everything: your sanity, your beliefs, your very existence. The world you knew is gone, shattered into a million pieces, and you're left picking through the wreckage. It's a dark, lonely place. I wouldn't wish it on my worst enemy, honestly. But here's the thing: somewhere, deep in that terrifying process, something shifts. Something unexpected starts to bloom, even if you can't see it yet. That something is gratitude. And it's not the kind of gratitude you're used to - not "thank you for the coffee" gratitude. This is primal, soul-deep thankfulness for simply existing, for breathing, for the sheer, brutal fact of being alive.

I remember the nights, the weeks, the months. I thought I was going insane. The energy, the visions, the physical sensations - it was relentless. Sleep was a distant memory. Food tasted like ash. My relationships crumbled because how do you explain this to anyone? They'd just stare at you, their eyes filled with pity or fear. You become an alien in your own life. You try to find answers, you desperately search for someone, anyone, who understands. You might stumble upon books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), and they offer glimmers of hope, a confirmation that you're not entirely alone in this cosmic joke. But even then, the fear is always there, a cold hand gripping your heart. You're not looking for gratitude then, you're looking for a way out. Right? You just want it to stop.

The Disintegration of "You"

Before any real gratitude can show up, you have to go through the fire. And it is fire. It burns away everything that isn't truly you. All those identities you've built up - your job, your relationships, your beliefs about yourself, your accomplishments - they start to dissolve. It's like your entire self-concept is being put through a shredder. You lose your bearings. You don't know who you are anymore, or what you're supposed to do. This is the ego's death, and it fights back with every ounce of its being. That's why it's so terrifying. It feels like you're dying, because a part of you is dying. The part that thought it was in control, the part that identified with all the superficial stuff. And that's a good thing, believe it or not. It's a painful, agonizingly slow good thing, but it's good.

I'll tell you what, that process stripped me bare. I was left with nothing but raw, exposed consciousness. No defenses, no pretense. Just me, and the overwhelming, often painful, energy moving through my body. There were moments I truly thought I wouldn't make it. The physical pain, the emotional agony, the sheer mental exhaustion - it felt endless. I'd curl up in a ball, just wishing for oblivion. But oblivion never came. Instead, the energy kept moving, pushing, purging. It forces you to confront every shadow, every fear, every unacknowledged trauma. You can't run. You can't hide. It's all there, in your face, demanding to be seen and felt. That's why so many of us end up feeling like we're having a The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) kind of experience, or even a Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It's not just a rough patch; it's a complete overhaul of your operating system, and it doesn't ask for permission.

The Cracks of Light

So, where does gratitude fit into this mess? It doesn't arrive like a sudden burst of sunshine. It's more like a tiny crack appearing in the concrete, letting in a sliver of light. You might not even recognize it at first. It's subtle. It's quiet. It's the moment you realize you just took a full, deep breath without thinking about it. Or you notice the way the light hits a leaf outside your window. Or you hear a bird sing, and for a split second, the incessant mental chatter quiets down. These aren't big, dramatic moments. They're tiny, fragile openings in the storm.

For me, it started with simple things. I remember one morning, after a night of intense tremors and fear, I woke up and the sun was shining. I mean, it was just the sun, doing what the sun does. But something in me just paused. And I felt a faint, almost imperceptible sense of "thank you." Not to anyone, not for anything specific. Just... thank you. For the light. For the warmth. For the simple fact that the world was still turning, even though my inner world felt like it was spinning out of control. It was a fleeting moment, quickly swallowed by the anxiety again, but it was there. A seed was planted.

The Slow Rebuild

As the energy starts to stabilize - and it will, I promise you, it won't last forever in that raw, terrifying state - you begin to see things differently. You're still raw, still tender, but the constant assault lessens. You start to feel your feet on the ground again, even if it's shaky ground. And that's when the gratitude really starts to take root. You've been through hell, you've faced your deepest fears, and you've come out the other side. You're not the same person you were, not even close. And that's where the unexpected thankfulness comes in.

You become grateful for the most basic things. Seriously. The feeling of cool water on your skin. The taste of a simple meal. The silence after a period of intense internal noise. The ability to just sit still for a few minutes without feeling like you're going to explode. These things, which you probably took for granted before, now feel like miracles. You realize how much beauty and simple pleasure exists in the mundane, in the ordinary, in the things you completely overlooked when you were busy chasing external goals and identities. Know what I mean? It's like your senses have been recalibrated. Everything is brighter, sharper, more vivid.

Gratitude for the Process Itself

This is the really wild part. Eventually, you might even find yourself feeling grateful for the crisis itself. I know, I know. It sounds insane. "Grateful for the thing that nearly broke me?" Yes. Because without that shattering, without that complete dismantling, you wouldn't be where you are now. You wouldn't have shed all the false layers. You wouldn't have discovered the resilience you never knew you possessed. You wouldn't have connected to something so much bigger than yourself.

It's like going through a spiritual boot camp. It's brutal, it's unforgiving, but it forces you to become stronger, clearer, more authentic. You learn what truly matters. You learn to let go of what doesn't. You learn to trust something beyond your logical mind. And that, my friend, is an invaluable gift. It's not something you can buy or learn from a book. It's something you earn through fire. That's why, even though I wouldn't wish the suffering on anyone, I wouldn't trade my experience for anything. It broke me open in a way nothing else could have. And through that brokenness, I found a connection, a depth of feeling, and a deep appreciation for life that I never knew was possible.

So yeah, it's not a "woo-woo" kind of gratitude. It's a hard-earned, gritty, deeply personal thankfulness. It's the gratitude of a survivor. It's the gratitude of someone who's seen the abyss and come back. And it changes everything. It changes how you interact with people, how you view your problems, how you experience joy and sorrow. It grounds you in a way you weren't before. Because you've been to the absolute bottom, and you've found something there - a spark, a truth, a resilience - that can never be taken away from you.

Living with Post-Crisis Gratitude

This isn't to say that life becomes a perpetual state of bliss. It doesn't. You'll still have bad days, you'll still face challenges, you'll still feel anger or sadness or frustration. But the lens through which you view those experiences shifts. You have a deeper well of inner resources to draw upon. You understand impermanence in a way you never could have before. You've seen things come apart and come back together, often in ways you couldn't have imagined.

Here's the thing: post-crisis gratitude isn't about ignoring the pain or pretending everything is perfect. It's about acknowledging the pain, walking through it, and still finding something to be thankful for. It's about the quiet appreciation for simply being, for the breath in your lungs, for the beating of your heart, for the sun on your face. It's a deep, abiding reverence for life itself, in all its messy, beautiful, terrifying glory. It's a quiet hum beneath the surface of your daily life, reminding you of the miracle of existence.

You'll find yourself noticing things you never did before. The texture of a cloud, the intricacy of a flower, the sound of rain on the roof. These aren't just observations; they're moments of connection, moments of deep appreciation. And this isn't just some intellectual exercise. It's a felt sense, a visceral experience. It changes your vibration, your energy. It makes you more present, more aware, more open. It’s not something you force; it arises naturally from the depth of your experience. Does that land? It's like your heart has been cracked open, and now it can hold so much more - more pain, yes, but also infinitely more beauty and love and thankfulness.

Finding Your Way Through

If you're still in the thick of it, feeling lost and terrified, just know that this gratitude, this deep appreciation, is waiting for you on the other side. It's part of the gift that this terrifying process eventually reveals. You just have to keep going. Keep breathing. Keep looking for those tiny cracks of light. Don't give up. Reach out for help if you can find it - someone who understands, someone who won't just dismiss what you're going through. You're not crazy. You're not alone. You're going through something unusual, something that's tearing you down to build you back up into something stronger, more real, more connected.

The truth is, this journey is messy, unpredictable, and often incredibly painful. There's no straight line, no easy answers. But there is an end to the crisis phase, and there is a deep transformation that comes from it. And with that transformation comes a kind of gratitude that can only be forged in the fire. It's not a superficial "happy-go-lucky" feeling; it's a deep, quiet, unwavering thankfulness for the miracle of being alive, for the lessons learned, for the deep shift in perspective. You'll look back and see how every single terrifying moment was actually leading you to a deeper, richer, more authentic connection with yourself and with life itself. And that, my friend, is truly something to be thankful for.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
